title: Disposable Email Domain Synthetic Identity Testing with Escalation
narrative: Fraudster uses throwaway email services (mailinator.com, guerrillamail.com, 10minutemail.com) to test stolen card data with low-value purchases before attempting larger fraud. Initial transaction at $1.99-$9.99 with P_emaildomain matching known disposable provider list. If approved, secondary transaction within 45-90 minutes jumps to $247-$899, often switching ProductCD from 'W' (digital) to 'H' (physical goods). R_emaildomain typically null or mismatched. Card shows card1_txn_count_1day=1 on first hit, then rapid card1_txn_count_6hr spike to 3-5. Disposable emails allow bypassing merchant fraud rules tied to email reputation scores. Addr1 often generic or high-risk zip codes. CVV match on first transaction establishes false confidence, enabling approval on larger follow-up.
indicators: P_emaildomain in disposable provider list (mailinator, guerrillamail, tempmail, 10minutemail, trashmail)
card1_txn_count_1day=1 on first transaction, card1_txn_count_6hr jumps to 3-5
R_emaildomain null or mismatched from P_emaildomain
ProductCD switches from 'W' to 'H' between transactions
card1_amt_zscore increases from <0.5 to >2.8 across transaction pair
CVV match on initial low-value test, may fail or be absent on escalation
summary: A card is tested with a small purchase under $10 using a temporary email address like mailinator.com or guerrillamail.com, then within 45-90 minutes the same card is used for a much larger purchase ($247-$899) that shifts from digital to physical goods.
reasoning: Fraudsters use disposable emails to avoid detection systems that track email reputation, testing stolen card details with a low-risk transaction to confirm the card works before quickly escalating to high-value fraud while the card is still active.
indicator_explanations: Email address uses a throwaway service known for fraud testing
First purchase is a small test, quickly followed by large fraudulent charge
Card goes from zero activity to multiple hits within hours
Recipient email missing or different from purchaser email address
Product type changes between the test transaction and escalation hit
Purchase amount rockets from normal to extreme for this card
Security code validates initially but fails or vanishes on bigger charge
